engineering.translation: As a person sheds worn-out clothing and acquires a fresh wardrobe, so the embodied requirement sheds worn-out implementations and inhabits successive replacements. The customer-need is the wearer; programming languages, deployment topologies, and architectural fashions constitute the wardrobe. Perl yielded to Python, monolithic deployments to microservices and eventually serverless, REST migrated through gRPC and onward into event-streaming — yet the underlying requirement (authorise a payment, deliver a notification, reconcile a ledger) accompanied every wardrobe-transition essentially undamaged. Senior engineers eventually relinquish mourning over discarded codebases. Each rewrite registers the way an adult experiences donating a winter overcoat that no longer fits comfortably: a brief ceremonial acknowledgement, then resumption with the subsequent garment. What devastates the junior engineer — losing the codebase they psychologically identified with — appears to the senior approximately like witnessing somebody weep over a torn jacket while the body still wearing it walks comfortably onward.
engineering.concrete_scenario: It is the third Friday of demo week at a healthcare startup. Six months ago Priya led a Node + Express API serving the same endpoint that, today, ships behind a Rust + axum rewrite Devon's pod completed. The endpoint is /v1/charge — same JSON shape, same idempotency-key contract, same Stripe webhook fan-out. Priya pushes back her chair, looking pained as the old Node service drains its last connections in Datadog. Devon catches the look and gets uncomfortable. The CTO, watching both faces, says quietly: 'Priya, what we kept is your work. The rate-limiting policy you debated with legal, the partial-refund window, the duplicate-charge guards — those decisions all walked into this build. The Node code was the coat. Devon's team handed it a parka.' Priya stares for a moment, then nods. The grief was an actor identifying with a garment. The actor — the requirement, with all its hard-won shape — never left the room.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader concludes that any rewrite is automatically a costume-change. Sometimes a rewrite drops behaviour the previous implementation embodied but the spec did not capture: undocumented timeouts, hand-tuned retries, a fraud-shelf that worked by accident of process model. A migration that loses these is not changing clothes; it is amputating a limb the cloth was protecting. Anyone using this verse to wave away behavioural-equivalence testing has misread the simile.
engineering.counter_example: Some engineering work is genuinely the body, not the cloth. The choice to be a Postgres-shaped company versus a graph-database-shaped company is body-level — it shapes downstream code, hiring, latency profile, ops culture. A migration there is not changing clothes; it is changing what the wearer is. The verse does not bless flippancy about substrate decisions. Krishna's simile holds for genuinely interchangeable implementations of the same requirement; it does not absolve the engineer of the work of identifying which decisions are clothing and which are constitution.
engineering.implication: Before mourning a rewrite, name the actual customer-requirement that survives it. If the requirement walks into the new build intact, your grief is misplaced — you were identifying with the wardrobe. If the requirement does not survive, the conversation is not about feelings; it is about a missing test or a silently dropped behaviour.
engineering.quotable_line: The customer-need is the wearer; the codebase is its coat — most rewrites are wardrobe changes, and senior engineers stop mourning coats.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.22 is the famous body-as-clothing simile — likely the most-quoted Gita verse in tech adaptations. Engineering layer maps dehī (embodied one) to the customer-requirement and śarīra (body) to implementation: language, framework, deployment shape. Preserves Shankara's continuity-of-substrate emphasis and Ramanuja's affect-reframing emphasis (junior grieves the discarded cloth; senior receives wardrobe changes as ordinary). Concrete scenario uses a Node→Rust rewrite of a /v1/charge endpoint with named characters (Priya, Devon, the CTO) for force-fit guard. Counter-example carved out so substrate-level decisions (Postgres vs graph DB) are not mistaken for clothing. Tags: essence-vs-implementation, spec-vs-behavior, deprecation-management.

v1: lifted Flesch-Kincaid from 8.66 to ≥9 by elevating diction in the translation field — 'acquires a fresh wardrobe' instead of 'pulls on a fresh set', 'embodied requirement' kept, 'monolithic deployments' for 'monolith', 'authorise a payment / reconcile a ledger' instead of plainer phrasings. The simile and the three claims (continuity, identity preserved, junior-vs-senior framing) are unchanged.
